name: Loving-Kindness Meditation
tagline: Warm up your heart by wishing yourself and others well.

origin.psychologist: Buddhist origin; clinical adaptation by Sharon Salzberg

situations: Wanting to feel more warmth and connection
summary: Loving-Kindness Meditation (metta) is the practice of silently offering goodwill — first to yourself, then outward to others, even difficult people. You repeat simple phrases like 'May you be happy, may you be safe' while letting the warmth grow. Over time it measurably increases positive emotion and feelings of connection.
howItWorks: We can deliberately cultivate warmth the way we cultivate any habit. By repeatedly generating goodwill toward yourself and progressively wider circles of people, loving-kindness practice strengthens the neural and emotional patterns of care and connection while softening hostility and self-criticism. Research shows it builds positive emotions over time, which in turn broadens social connection and well-being. Including difficult people gently loosens resentment.

steps.instruction: Sit comfortably. Bring to mind the feeling of being cared for.
Offer the phrases to yourself: 'May I be happy. May I be healthy. May I be safe. May I live with ease.'
steps.mantra: May I be happy and at ease.

steps.instruction: Extend the same wishes to someone you love, then to a neutral person.
If you're willing, extend them to a difficult person, then to all beings.
steps.note: Start small with the difficult person; don't force it.

whenToUse: When you feel disconnected or lonely
Building a baseline of warmth over weeks
whenNotToUse: If directing kindness at yourself brings up strong distress, start with a loved one
To bypass or 'spiritually skip over' legitimate anger
As pressure to forgive someone before you're ready
tips: Use phrases that feel sincere to you — adapt the wording
Lead with a loved one if self-directed kindness is hard at first
Let the warmth be felt, not just recited

evidence.summary: Loving-kindness and compassion meditation have meta-analytic support for increasing positive emotions and reducing self-criticism and some symptoms of depression and anxiety.
citations: Galante, J. et al. (2014). Loving-kindness meditation effects on well-being: a meta-analysis. JCCP.
